TipCppreference is a reference for both C++ and C. Since C++ shares some function names with C, you may find yourself in the C reference after searching for something. The URL and the navigation bar at the top of cppreference always show you if you’re browsing the C or C++ reference.

content.text: TipWhen you finderaseon cppreference, you can ignore the overloads that use iterators.
When you finderaseon cppreference, you can ignore the overloads that use iterators.
TipIndexes in C++ start at 0. The character at index 0 in the string “House” is ‘H’, at 1 it’s ‘o’, and so on.
Indexes in C++ start at 0. The character at index 0 in the string “House” is ‘H’, at 1 it’s ‘o’, and so on.

Show SolutionThe kingHere’s how you’d get there by using the search function on cppreference (you might have skipped the first step by using a search engine):Searching for stringand clicking “std::string” leads us tostd::basic_string.Scrolling to the “Member functions” list, we finderase. As hinted in the tip above, the firstfunction overloadis used. It takes 2size_type(Unsigned integer type) arguments. In our example, 4 and 11. According to the description of (1), it removes “min(count, size() - index)characters starting atindex“. Plugging in our arguments, it removesmin(11, 19 - 4) = 11characters starting at index 4.
